title: Industrial Light & Magic
content:
{{Top|rwc}}
{{Youmay|the real-world company [[Industrial Light & Magic]]|the [[in-universe]] company [[Interstellar Lightspeed Manufacturing]]|linebreak=1}}
{{Multiple_issues|citation|cleanup|expand}}
{{RealCompany
|image=[[File:ILMlogo.svg]]
|name=Industrial Light & Magic
|founded=[[1975]]<ref>{{Lucasfilm|url=what-we-do/visual-effects|text=Visual Effects}}</ref>
|founder=[[George Lucas]]
|dissolved=
|headquarters=
|parent=*[[Lucasfilm]]
**Lucas Digital {{C|formerly}}
|subsid=[[ILM Immersive]]
|website=[https://www.ilm.com ilm.com]
}}
{{Quote|The feeling around about the place was that now we were established, that we had a life of our own, so to speak. Instead of thinking we were hired for ''Empire'' and would never, ever do another job up there, there was a sense of excitement. And there was a real trust placed in all of us, too, by George ''[Lucas]'' and the other filmmakers who would come in. We were allowed to have authorship on our work. We were there because we actually brought something to the work. We weren't just middlemen handing jobs off to a zillion other people. And we were there because our work was appreciated. We loved our crews. We gave them as much authorship as we could. We had our voice and then we were representing the director. And it was a very clean way to work.|[[Ken Ralston]]|<ref name="221 Insider">{{InsiderCite|221|Here We Go Again}}</ref>}}
'''Industrial Light & Magic''' ('''ILM''') is the visual effects company responsible for much of the visual effects in the ''[[Star Wars]]'' films. It was founded by [[George Lucas]] in [[1975]], as part of [[Lucasfilm]]. ILM has also created the visual effects for over 300 other films, including the [[Wikipedia:List of Star Trek films|''Star Trek'']] films, the [[Wikipedia:Indiana Jones (franchise)|''Indiana Jones'']] franchise, the [[Wikipedia:Back to the Future (franchise)|''Back to the Future'']] trilogy, the [[Wikipedia:Jurassic Park|''Jurassic Park'']] series, the [[Wikipedia:Harry Potter|''Harry Potter'']] series, the [[Wikipedia:Pirates of the Caribbean (film series)|''Pirates of the Caribbean'']] series, many films in the [[Wikipedia:Marvel Cinematic Universe|''Marvel Cinematic Universe'']], the 2011 animated film [[Wikipedia:Rango (2011 film)|''Rango'']], the 2011 live-action film [[Wikipedia:Super 8 (film)|''Super 8'']], and the 2018 film [[Wikipedia:Ready Player One (film)|''Ready Player One'']].

==History==
===Creation===
[[File:ILMATST-SWFB.jpg|thumb|left|220px|ILM with a model of an [[All Terrain Scout Transport/Legends|AT-ST]]]]
After [[George Lucas]] made the hit movie [[Wikipedia:American Graffiti|''American Graffiti'']], he worked on a space opera he called ''The Star Wars'', which was then purchased by [[20th Century Studios|20th Century Fox]]. Lucas intended to create special effects that had never been done before.<ref name="SW">{{SWArchive|url=themovies/saga/19990715news.html|text=Industrial Light & Magic: History}}</ref> However, Fox's in-house special effects department had been shut down because of costs and the public's interest in more realistic looking films. Lucas then decided to create his own effects company. His first choice was Douglas Trumbull, responsible for the photographical effects in ''2001: A Space Odyssey''. As Trumbull was doing [[Steven Spielberg]]'s ''Close Encounters of the Third Kind'', he instead recommended Lucas his assistant, [[John Dykstra]]. Dykstra then assembled a crew of 75 college students, artists and engineers. Lucas himself bought old equipment for pennies on the dollar.

[[Gary Kurtz]] bough to crew to work on a warehouse in Van Nuys, California. Looking for a name that would disguise the warehouse's function and suggest it was simply in the business of wholesaling electronic components rather than making movies, Lucas came up with Industrial Light and Magic. In May of 1975, the company was born.<ref>{{YouTube|video=aeIXcIwJ-Zo|text=Interviewing Return of the Jedi Lucasfilm VFX Editor Bill Kimberlin - Rule of Two|channel=Star Wars Theory|name=@StarWarsTheory}}</ref>

===''Star Wars'' work===
[[File:OriginalILMlogoStruzan-MOTESB.png|thumb|200px|The original ILM logo, designed by [[Drew Struzan]]]]
ILM's first film was [[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|''Star Wars'']] (called ''The Star Wars'' at first), which became the first film of the [[original trilogy]]. Many at the recently founded company (then jokingly nicknamed "The Country Club") were not sure the new groundbreaking special effects would ever work. The working environment was chaotic and unorthodox: when Fox executives visited the crew to see their progress, one of the employees was wearing a fish-head mask, the artists used an improvised slide to plunge into a swimming pool built in a container, and Dykstra threw a refrigerator from the warehouse's roof "because we wanted to know how it would sound." The artists spent their days smoking marijuana and trying to find relief from the hot environment in bathtubs. Most of the $1 million Lucas lent to the company was spent on equipment, such as miniatures and the [[Wikipedia:Dykstraflex|Dykstraflex]] motion control camera — a technology later crucial for scenes such as the [[Battle of Yavin/Legends|Battle of Yavin]].

When Lucas returned to California after ending principal photography, ILM was in worse shape than ever before. Except for the [[Escape pod/Legends|escape pod]] being released from [[Tantive IV/Legends|''Tantive IV'']], Lucas found all the completed visual effects to be unsalvageable. Adding this to the troublesome shooting, most doubted ''Star Wars'' would ever reach theaters, and Fox nearly terminated production. Lucas decided to take direct control of the company, supervising the effects work daily. The battle scenes were mostly directly copied from dogfights taken from World War II movies such as ''Tora! Tora! Tora!'' which Lucas used as reference.

The physical [[starship]] models of the original trilogy were made to emulate the esthetics of World War II, with their sections riveted together; the method was called "boilerplate technology" by the company's model shop.<ref name="The Secrets of Star Wars: Shadows of the Empire">''[[The Secrets of Star Wars: Shadows of the Empire]]''</ref>

Lucas also brought in some third parties to complete visual effects, such as [[Dan O'Bannon]], [[Rick Baker]] and [[Phil Tippett]].<ref name="SW-We Meet Again At Last: ILM Veterans Reunite to Celebrate 40 Years of ''Star Wars''">{{SW|url=news/ilm-reunion-40-years-of-star-wars|text=We Meet Again At Last: ILM Veterans Reunite to Celebrate 40 Years of ''Star Wars''|date=2017-06-14}}</ref><ref name="SW-From Space Battles to StageCraft: The Legends of ILM Discuss Half a Century of Movie Magic">{{SW|url=news/legends-of-ilm-interview-light-and-magic|text=From Space Battles to StageCraft: The Legends of ILM Discuss Half a Century of Movie Magic|date=2022-08-18}}</ref>

Once the film was completed, most of the crew at ILM decided to stay, but Dykstra brought some of the artists to form his own company, called Apogee.

Although ILM primarily worked on ''Star Wars'' projects, they also had a hand in some ''Star Trek'' projects, including ''Star Trek II: Wrath of Khan''. Mark Hamill, upon discovering this during production of ''[[Return of the Jedi]]'', jokingly referred to them as traitors, with Lucas stating that it was a business.<ref name="Making of ROTJ">''[[The Making of Return of the Jedi]]''</ref>

Through the mid-1980s, ILM began to change the entire special effects industry forever by experimenting with Computer Graphics. after the success with [[wikipedia:Tron|Tron]], Lucas became interested with computers and saw the potential of the new revolutionary machine. Lucas hired [[Edwin Catmull|Ed Catmill]], a PHD student, whom he would bring more computer scientists, technologists, etc. into the visual effects department. along with Catmill's early advancements in computer technology, was the ability to create smooth surfaces and wrap textures around them. this was a pioneering leap in the rudimentary world of [[wikipedia:Computer graphics|computer graphics]], known simply as CG.<ref>{{WebCite|url=https://www.cs.cmu.edu/~ph/nyit/morrison/1970s.txt|text=1970's - School of Computer Science - Carnegie Mellon School of Computer Science|archivedate=20230815205234}}</ref><ref>{{WebCite|url=https://books.google.no/books?id=fnBqEAAAQBAJ&pg=PT170&dq=Sprockets+Systems+Research+and+Development+Lucasfilm+EditDroid+THX&hl=no&newbks=1&newbks_redir=0&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiQs46jnZGBAxVfQvEDHTQCDzUQ6AF6BAgIEAI#v=onepage&q=Sprockets%20Systems%20Research%20and%20Development%20Lucasfilm%20EditDroid%20THX&f=false|text=The Empire of Effects: Industrial Light and Magic and the Rendering of Realism|author=Julie A. Turnock|date=2022-06-14|work=University of Texas Press|archivedate=20230907220736}}</ref><ref>{{WebCite|url=https://www.pixar.com/our-story-pixar|text=Pixar Animation Studios|work=Pixar Animation Studios|archivedate=20230911212730}}</ref> ILM created the [[wikipedia:OpenEXR|OpenEXR]] format for [[wikipedia:high-dynamic-range imaging|high-dynamic-range imaging]].<ref name="About OpenEXR">{{WebCite|url=http://www.openexr.com/about.html|text=About OpenEXR|work=ILM|archivedate=20060703004922|nolive=1}}</ref>

In the '90s ILM's work was mostly based on [[Wikipedia:Softimage 3D|Softimage 3D]], but the company conceived the development of a "virtual studio" of their own proprietary technology, that would allow the inhouse programmers to update and modify it according to the company's needs. They began developing "Zeno" as a foundation of modular tool-building. Zeno saw first limited use in [[2000]] and [[2003]], demonstrating its use not only in animation but also rigid-body dynamics and [[Wikipedia:Match moving|match moving]].<ref name="artunleashed">''[[The Art and Making of Star Wars: The Force Unleashed]]''</ref>

For [[Star Wars: Episode I The Phantom Menace|''Star Wars'': Episode I ''The Phantom Menace'']], the visual effects team used a series of character animation tools and lip-sync refence materials to infuse new digital characters with dead-on accurate dialogue, and dynamic physical behaviors. The film was shot completely on high-definition cameras. For the first time, [[Yoda]] was going to appear digitally. Artist Jean Bolte used Yoda from the original trilogy as reference material and focused on getting Yoda's expression right. The Yoda puppet was brought in for the actors to perform with, then, they replace the puppet with their CGI Yoda for the films.

In [[2002]], ILM and [[Skywalker Sound]] produced the cinematics for the [[LucasArts]] [[Star Wars video games|video game]] ''[[Star Wars: Bounty Hunter]]''.<ref name="artunleashed" />

By [[2004]], Zeno had developed enough allowing ILM to replace its old toolset and pipeline and use Zeno with [[Wikipedia:Autodesk Maya|Autodesk Maya]] and Photoshop. [[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith|''Star Wars'': Episode III ''Revenge of the Sith'']] took advantage of this new framework (before it, Spielberg's [[Wikipedia:War of the Worlds (2005 film)|''War of the Worlds'']] was the first film to do so).<ref name="artunleashed" />

ILM has one of the largest render farms (named [[wikipedia:Death Star (ILM)|Death Star]]). It is a cluster computer originally built by SGI. Since then, it has been converted into a Linux system built by RackSaver (now Verari Systems) with AMD processors.<ref>{{WebCite|url=http://www.linuxjournal.com/article/6783|text=ILM death star render farm|archivedate=20230330112648}}</ref> The system originally had 1,500 processors in 750 nodes, this figure was doubled for ''Revenge of the Sith''. ILM tends to be quiet about their supercomputer, so its current configuration is not known.

In 2005, Industrial Light and Magic moved from [[wikipedia:Kerner Optical|Kerner Optical]] to [[wikipedia:Presidio of San Francisco|the Presidio of San Francisco]]. There, the company worked in the same place with engineers of [[LucasArts]] during the development of ''[[Star Wars: The Force Unleashed]]''. The two teams worked closely and LucasArts built "Zed", a game editor that used Zeno's toolset, and integrated their technologies into a single tool pipeline.<ref name="artunleashed" /> ILM's face- and motion-capture "CloneCam" technology (previously used for the ''[[Wikipedia:Pirates of the Caribbean (film series)|Pirates of the Caribbean]]'' movies) recorded actors' voice and physical performances for the game characters.<ref>{{WebCite|url-status=dead|url=http://tech.uk.msn.com/gaming/article.aspx?cp-documentid=9092634|text=The technology of The Force Unleashed|date=2008-08-01|work=tech & gadgets|archivedate=20080826070412}}</ref><ref name="SWArchive-Casting and Capturing Captivating Performances">{{SWArchive|url=gaming/videogames/news/f20070918/index.html|text=Casting and Capturing Captivating Performances|author=W. Haden Blackman}}</ref>

[[File:ILMold.svg|thumb|left|200px|Older logo]]
In 2010, ILM allowed the show [[Wikipedia:The Amazing Race|''The Amazing Race'']] to film at their complex. They set up a challenge for the contestants on the show, which involved the characters of [[Ponds]], [[Cody]], and [[Padmé Amidala]] from [[Star Wars: The Clone Wars|''The Clone Wars'']].

In October 2012, [[The Walt Disney Company|Disney]] bought ILM's parent company, [[Lucasfilm]], acquiring ILM in the process.<ref>{{WebCite|url=https://variety.com/2012/film/news/disney-buys-lucasfilm-new-star-wars-planned-1118061434|text=Disney Buys LucasFilm, New 'Star Wars' Planned|date=2012-10-30|work=Variety|archivedate=20230415204550}}</ref><ref>{{WebCite|url=https://deadline.com/2012/10/disney-acquires-lucasfilm-star-wars-creator-362389|text=UPDATE: BREAKING: 'Star Wars' Returns – 'Episode 7' Slated For 2015 And More Movies Planned As Disney Buys Lucasfilm|date=2022-10-30|work=Deadline Hollywood|archivedate=20230716100936}}</ref><ref>{{WebCite|url=https://www.latimes.com/entertainment/envelope/la-xpm-2012-oct-30-la-et-ct-disney-lucasfilm-20121030-story.html|text=Disney to acquire Lucasfilm for $4.05 billion|date=2012-10-30|work=Los Angeles Times|archivedate=20230619090641}}</ref><ref>{{WebCite|url=https://ew.com/article/2012/10/30/disney-buying-lucasfilm-new-star-wars-film|text=Disney buying Lucasfilm, prepping new 'Star Wars' movies for 2015 and beyond -- VIDEO|date=2012-10-30|work=Entertainment Weekly|archivedate=20221205043914}}</ref>

ILM TV, launched in 2018, is a branch of ILM that focuses on episodic and streaming television. It collaborates with the [[ILM Immersive|ILM Experience Lab]], and worked on the live action ''Star Wars'' series ''[[Star Wars: The Mandalorian]]''. Aside from ''The Mandalorian'', it will also help create the second season of ''Krypton'' on SyFy. Their headquarters is located at ILM's London studio.<ref>{{WebCite|url=https://www.theverge.com/2018/11/7/18072304/industrial-light-magic-tv-division-star-wars-marvel|text=Industrial Light & Magic opens TV division just in time for Disney's new Star Wars show|author=Bishop, Bryan|date=2018-11-07|work=The Verge|archivedate=20200423200239}}</ref>

in 2019, ILM introduced [[wikipedia:StageCraft|StageCraft]].<ref>{{WebCite|url=https://deadline.com/2021/07/stagecraft-ilm-disney-plus-mandalorian-vfx-cannes-magazine-disruptor-1234787530|text=How ILM's Stagecraft Team Is Pushing The Boundaries Of VFX And "Moving The Tech Forward Right Now"|date=2021-07-08|work=Deadline Hollywood|archivedate=20221226004901}}</ref><ref>{{WebCite|url=https://postperspective.com/the-evolution-of-icvfx-ilm-stagecraft-and-dimension|text=The Evolution of ICVFX: ILM Stagecraft and Dimension|date=2022-07-20|work=postPerspective|archivedate=20220722042734}}</ref> Also known as "The Volume", it uses high-definition LED video walls to generate virtual sceneries, all of this was done using the graphically impressive video game engine; [[wikipedia:Unreal Engine|Unreal Engine 5]]. it was first used in [[Star Wars: The Mandalorian]] (2019-present).<ref>{{WebCite|url=https://www.slashfilm.com/955412/how-ilms-volume-technology-reinvents-visual-effects-and-what-it-means-for-the-future|text=How ILM's Volume Technology Reinvents Visual Effects (And What It Means For The Future)|date=2022-08-07|work=/Film|archivedate=20230912194516}}</ref><ref>{{WebCite|url=https://collider.com/industrial-light-magic-what-is-the-volume-stagecraft-technology|text=Industrial Light & Magic's Digital StageCraft Technology: What We Know About the Volume|date=2022-08-14|work=Collider|archivedate=20230519142114}}</ref>

==Notable artists==
*[[John Dykstra]] founded ILM, leading a team that included [[Richard Edlund]], [[Dennis Muren]], and future director [[Joe Johnston]].
*[[Edwin Catmull]]'s [[Wikipedia:Pixar|Pixar]] was at first the computer graphics department of ILM.
*The famous digital artist [[Juan-Luis Sanchez]] worked for ILM between [[2001]] and [[2006]], during this time working on both [[Star Wars: Episode II Attack of the Clones|''Star Wars'': Episode II ''Attack of the Clones'']] and [[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith|''Star Wars'': Episode III ''Revenge of the Sith'']].
*[[Steve Williams]] was also an animator for ILM.
*[[Masi Oka]] worked at ILM before becoming an actor.
*[[Chris Voy]], art director for [[Star Wars: Episode IX The Rise of Skywalker|''Star Wars'': Episode IX ''The Rise of Skywalker'']].
*[[Stephen Zavala]], senior concept artist for ''Star Wars'': Episode IX ''The Rise of Skywalker''.
